# Chapter 4: Training Data — Augmented Summary

*Designing Machine Learning Systems* (Chip Huyen). Generated by the Augmented Summary pipeline (`Augmented-Summary-System/PROCESS-SPEC.md`).

> **Where it sits:** Chapter 3 covered how to *store and move* data; Chapter 4 is about turning raw data into a *training set* — sampling it, labeling it, balancing it, and augmenting it. It feeds directly into Chapter 5 (Feature Engineering) and Chapter 6 (Model Development & Evaluation). The chapter's recurring theme: training data is a craft full of pitfalls, not a solved input.

**Legend:** ◀ backward · ▶ forward · ↔ lateral/jump · ⧉ external · 🎯 difficulty · 🛠 hands-on · ✅ verified expansion

---

## Part 1 — Reading-order summary with associations

### Sampling

**§ Sampling (intro)** 🎯 Intro
Why sampling happens everywhere: you sample to create training data from real-world data, and again to create splits. Good sampling can be the difference between a working and a failing model.
*Associations:* ◀ data sources & formats (Ch 3) · ▶ train/val/test splits (Ch 6)

**§ Nonprobability Sampling** 🎯 Intro
Samples chosen by convenience, snowball, judgment, or quota — not randomized. Cheap but riddled with selection bias; the data isn't representative of the population.
*Associations:* ⧉ Heckman 1979, "Sample Selection Bias as a Specification Error" (the formal warning) · ⧉ Lerman 2016 (self-driving-car convenience example) · ▶ data distribution shift (Ch 9)

**§ Simple Random Sampling** 🎯 Intro
Every example has equal selection probability. Simple, but rare classes can be missed entirely.
*Associations:* ▶ class imbalance (this chapter)

**§ Stratified Sampling** 🎯 Intro
Split the population into groups (strata) and sample each, guaranteeing rare groups appear.
*Associations:* ↔ class imbalance handling (this chapter) · ▶ evaluation by slices (Ch 6)

**§ Weighted Sampling** 🎯 Intermediate
Assign each sample a selection weight so some are drawn more often — useful to encode domain priors or correct distributions. Distinct from sample weights in the loss function (which the chapter contrasts).
*Associations:* ⧉ scikit-learn "SVM: Weighted Samples" · ↔ cost-sensitive learning (this chapter)

**§ Reservoir Sampling** 🎯 Intermediate
Elegant algorithm for sampling *k* items from a stream of unknown length in one pass, each with equal probability. Crucial for streaming data where you can't hold everything.
*Associations:* ◀ streaming data (Ch 3) · 🛠 implement the algorithm (see deep dives is not needed — it's fully covered in-chapter)

**§ Importance Sampling** 🎯 Advanced *(named, not derived → deep dive)*
Estimate properties of one distribution using samples from another, reweighting by the density ratio. The chapter uses it conceptually (off-policy reward reweighting) but doesn't give the estimator.
*Associations:* ⧉ Owen, *Monte Carlo Theory* · ↔ distribution shift reweighting (Ch 9) · ▶ **Deep dive #5**

### Labeling

**§ Labeling (intro)** 🎯 Intro
Most ML in industry is supervised, so labels are the bottleneck. The chapter frames the whole "how do I get labels" problem.
*Associations:* ▶ natural labels, weak supervision, semi-supervision, transfer learning, active learning (all below)

**§ Hand Labels** 🎯 Intro
Human annotation: expensive, slow, raises privacy concerns, and suffers from label multiplicity (annotator disagreement) and the need for clear label schemas. Data lineage matters when mixing label sources.
*Associations:* ▶ natural labels (next) · ↔ data lineage (Ch 3)

**§ Natural Labels** 🎯 Intermediate
Tasks where the system gets ground truth automatically from user behavior (e.g., did the user click / the ETA was right). Discusses feedback loop length and the difference between explicit and implicit feedback.
*Associations:* ⧉ Ktena et al. 2019 (delayed feedback in CTR) · ▶ continual learning & production labeling (Ch 9) · ▶ feedback loops (Ch 9)

**§ Handling the Lack of Labels (intro)** 🎯 Intro
Four families to cope with too few labels: weak supervision, semi-supervision, transfer learning, active learning. Introduced as a comparison table.

**§ Weak Supervision** 🎯 Advanced *(label model deferred → deep dive)*
Programmatic labeling: write *labeling functions* (heuristics) and let a system combine their noisy votes into probabilistic labels. Snorkel is the canonical system. The chapter shows LF examples but treats the label-combining model as a black box.
*Associations:* ⧉ Ratner et al., Snorkel (PVLDB 2017) · ⧉ Dunnmon et al. 2020 (cross-modal, medical) · ▶ **Deep dive #1**

**§ Semi-Supervision** 🎯 Advanced
Use a small labeled set plus a large unlabeled set. Classic method: self-training (label your own confident predictions). Also perturbation-based and graph-based methods.
*Associations:* ◀ weak supervision (prior) · ⧉ Zhu 2008 SSL survey **[in folder]** · ⧉ van Engelen & Hoos 2020 SSL survey **[in folder]** · ⧉ Blum & Mitchell 1998 (co-training) · ⧉ Oliver et al. 2018 (realistic evaluation caveat) · ↔ perturbation augmentation (this chapter, via Miyato VAT)

**§ Transfer Learning** 🎯 Intermediate
Pretrain on a data-rich task, then fine-tune (or zero/few-shot) on your low-label task. The chapter's examples are the foundation-model lineage.
*Associations:* ⧉ Howard & Ruder 2018 (ULMFiT) · ⧉ Devlin et al. 2018 (BERT) · ⧉ Brown et al. 2020 (GPT-3, few-shot) · ⧉ Liu et al. 2021 (prompting survey) · ▶ feature extraction & embeddings (Ch 5)

**§ Active Learning** 🎯 Intermediate
Let the model choose which examples to label next — query the most informative (most uncertain, or most disagreed-upon by a committee). Can hit target accuracy with far fewer labels.
*Associations:* ⧉ Settles 2010 Active Learning survey **[in folder]** · ⧉ Settles 2012 *Active Learning* book · ⧉ Angluin 1988 (query learning theory) · ↔ uncertainty/ensembles (Ch 6)

### Class Imbalance

**§ Class Imbalance (intro)** 🎯 Intro
When one class vastly outnumbers another. Common and consequential (fraud, disease, spam, resume screening).
*Associations:* ◀ simple random sampling misses rare classes (prior) · ⧉ Ng 2020 (production gap) · ⧉ Nilson/WebWire/Talos (real imbalance stats)

**§ Challenges of Class Imbalance** 🎯 Intermediate
Three challenges: too few minority signals to learn from; easy to fall into a non-useful majority-only solution; asymmetric cost of errors. Imbalance is worse with high problem complexity and small data.
*Associations:* ⧉ Johnson & Khoshgoftaar 2019 survey **[in folder]** · ⧉ Japkowicz 2000 & Japkowicz & Stephen 2002 (complexity interaction) · ⧉ Ding et al. 2017 (deep nets still struggle)

**§ Handling Class Imbalance → Right Metrics** 🎯 Intermediate
Accuracy is misleading under imbalance; use precision, recall, F1, and especially **precision-recall curves** over ROC for heavy skew.
*Associations:* ⧉ Davis & Goadrich 2006 (PR vs ROC) · ▶ evaluation metrics (Ch 6)

**§ Handling Class Imbalance → Data-Level Methods (resampling)** 🎯 Intermediate *(SMOTE/undersampling mechanics deferred → deep dive)*
Oversample the minority (SMOTE) or undersample the majority (Tomek links, one-sided selection); dynamic and two-phase schemes for deep nets. Resample only the training split.
*Associations:* ⧉ Chawla et al. 2002 (SMOTE) · ⧉ Tomek 1976 · ⧉ Kubat & Matwin 2000 (one-sided selection) · ⧉ Zhang & Mani 2003 · ⧉ Lee et al. 2016, Pouyanfar et al. 2018 (deep) · ⧉ Alencar (Kaggle tutorial) · ▶ **Deep dive #3**

**§ Handling Class Imbalance → Algorithm-Level Methods** 🎯 Advanced *(focal loss formula deferred → deep dive)*
Modify the loss instead of the data: cost-sensitive learning (cost matrix), class-balanced loss, and focal loss (down-weight easy examples). Ensembles also help.
*Associations:* ⧉ Elkan 2001 (cost-sensitive foundations) · ⧉ Cui et al. 2019 (class-balanced loss) · ⧉ Lin et al. 2017 (focal loss) · ⧉ Galar et al. 2012 (ensembles) · ▶ ensembles (Ch 6) · ▶ **Deep dive #2**

### Data Augmentation

**§ Data Augmentation (intro)** 🎯 Intro
Increase training data size/diversity to improve generalization and robustness. Three families follow.
*Associations:* ⧉ Shorten & Khoshgoftaar 2019 (image augmentation survey) **[in folder]**

**§ Simple Label-Preserving Transformations** 🎯 Intro
Flips, crops, rotations, color changes (vision); word/synonym replacement (NLP). Cheap and effective.
*Associations:* ⧉ Krizhevsky et al. 2012 (AlexNet popularized this) · ▶ feature engineering (Ch 5)

**§ Perturbation** 🎯 Advanced *(adversarial mechanics partly external)*
Add noise/adversarial perturbations to harden models. Tiny changes can flip predictions (fragility), so training on perturbed data improves robustness.
*Associations:* ⧉ Su et al. 2019 (one-pixel attack) · ⧉ Goodfellow et al. 2015 (FGSM, adversarial training) · ⧉ Moosavi-Dezfooli et al. 2016 (DeepFool) · ⧉ Miyato et al. 2017 (virtual adversarial training) · ↔ semi-supervision (VAT bridges both)

**§ Data Synthesis** 🎯 Advanced *(mixup/VRM deferred → deep dive)*
Synthesize training data: mixup (combine examples and labels) and GAN-based synthesis (e.g., CycleGAN for medical CT).
*Associations:* ⧉ Zhang et al. 2018 (mixup) · ⧉ Sandfort et al. 2019 (CycleGAN CT) · ▶ **Deep dive #4** · ↔ GANs (generative models)

---

## Part 2 — Deep dives (out-of-scope expansions)

> Produced by the `ml-concept-expander` sub-agent and checked by `expansion-verifier`. ✅ = verification passed (citation corrections applied).

### Deep dive #1 — Weak supervision label model (Snorkel) ✅ 🎯 Advanced
In *data programming*, instead of hand-labeling, domain experts write **labeling functions (LFs)** — small programs that emit a noisy vote (a class label or *abstain*) over each unlabeled example. LFs encode heuristics, regex patterns, knowledge-base lookups, or third-party model outputs. Individually they are biased, of unknown accuracy, and can correlate or conflict. The core problem is combining *m* LF outputs λ₁(x),…,λₘ(x) into a single probabilistic label ỹ = P(y | λ(x)) without any ground truth. Snorkel solves this with a **generative (label) model**: a probabilistic model P_θ(λ, y) — typically a factor graph whose parameters θ capture each LF's latent accuracy (and optionally pairwise correlations). Because *y* is never observed, θ is estimated by maximizing the *marginal* likelihood of the observed LF votes, θ̂ = argmax_θ Σ_x log Σ_y P_θ(λ(x), y). The key identifiability insight: agreements and disagreements among LFs alone reveal their relative accuracies — a more accurate LF agrees with other accurate LFs more often. The fitted model outputs *soft* probabilistic labels P_θ̂(y | λ(x)), which train a downstream "noise-aware" discriminative model (e.g., a deep net) with a soft cross-entropy loss that generalizes beyond the LFs' coverage. The payoff: experts inject supervision programmatically and update it cheaply when requirements change, rather than re-labeling.
**Key references:** Ratner, De Sa, Wu, Selsam, Ré, "Data Programming: Creating Large Training Sets, Quickly," NeurIPS 2016 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1605.07723. Ratner, Bach, Ehrenberg, Fries, Wu, Ré, "Snorkel: Rapid Training Data Creation with Weak Supervision," *Proc. VLDB Endow.* 11(3), 2017 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1711.10160.
🛠 **Hands-on:** Install `snorkel`; follow the Spam tutorial — write `@labeling_function`s, apply with `PandasLFApplier`, inspect coverage/conflict via `LFAnalysis`, then `LabelModel.fit()` and read off `predict_proba`.
**Prerequisites:** probabilistic graphical models, maximum likelihood / EM, latent variables, cross-entropy.

### Deep dive #2 — Focal loss ✅ 🎯 Intermediate
Focal loss reshapes cross-entropy to combat extreme class imbalance, particularly the foreground-background imbalance in dense object detection where easy background examples vastly outnumber objects. Let p_t be the model's probability of the *true* class (p_t = p if y=1, else 1−p). Cross-entropy is CE(p_t) = −log(p_t). Even well-classified examples (p_t ≈ 0.9) carry non-trivial loss, and tens of thousands of them dominate the gradient and swamp rare hard positives. **Focal loss** adds a modulating factor: **FL(p_t) = −(1−p_t)^γ · log(p_t)**. The factor (1−p_t)^γ is ≈1 for misclassified examples (small p_t) but shrinks toward 0 as p_t → 1, down-weighting easy examples. The focusing parameter γ ≥ 0 controls strength: γ=0 recovers cross-entropy, and γ=2 works well — a well-classified example with p_t=0.9 gets (0.1)² = 100× less loss. A class-balancing term α_t ∈ [0,1] is usually folded in: FL = −α_t (1−p_t)^γ log(p_t). The value is that focus shifts *dynamically* by difficulty (which evolves during training) rather than by a static class weight alone. RetinaNet, trained with focal loss, matched two-stage detectors' accuracy.
**Key reference:** Lin, Goyal, Girshick, He, Dollár, "Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection," ICCV 2017 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1708.02002.
🛠 **Hands-on:** Use `torchvision.ops.sigmoid_focal_loss` (γ=2, α=0.25) on an imbalanced binary classifier; compare PR-AUC vs. `BCEWithLogitsLoss`; sweep γ ∈ {0,1,2,5}.
**Prerequisites:** cross-entropy / log loss, sigmoid/softmax, class imbalance, gradients.

### Deep dive #3 — SMOTE and the resampling family ✅ 🎯 Intermediate
SMOTE over-samples the rare class not by duplicating points but by **interpolating** new ones. For a minority point x_i, pick one of its *k* nearest minority neighbors x_zi and create a synthetic example along the segment between them: **x_new = x_i + λ·(x_zi − x_i), λ ∼ U(0,1)**. This populates the convex hull of the minority class with plausible examples, widening minority decision regions and reducing the overfitting plain duplication causes. The *resampling family* combines over- and under-sampling. **Tomek links**: a pair of opposite-class points with no third example closer to either than they are to each other (i.e., effectively each other's nearest neighbor); removing the majority member of each link cleans the boundary. **One-sided selection (OSS)** combines Tomek links with the **Condensed Nearest Neighbor** rule to drop redundant majority examples far from the boundary. Common recipes: **SMOTE+Tomek** or **SMOTE+ENN** (synthesize, then clean). The **standard caution**: resample **only the training split, after** the train/test (or per-fold) split — resampling beforehand leaks information (synthetic points interpolated from instances that land in the test set, or duplicates on both sides) and inflates metrics. Grounded in Johnson & Khoshgoftaar (2019) **[in folder]**.
**Key references:** Chawla, Bowyer, Hall, Kegelmeyer, "SMOTE," *JAIR* 16 (2002) 321–357 — https://doi.org/10.1613/jair.953.
🛠 **Hands-on:** `imbalanced-learn`: `SMOTE`, `TomekLinks`/`OneSidedSelection`, `SMOTETomek`; wrap in `imblearn.pipeline.Pipeline` so resampling stays inside CV folds. Try the `creditcard` fraud dataset.
**Prerequisites:** k-NN, class imbalance, train/test leakage, decision boundaries.

### Deep dive #4 — mixup and Vicinal Risk Minimization ✅ 🎯 Intermediate
Standard training uses **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)**, approximating the data distribution by Dirac deltas at the training points — the model is constrained only *at* those points and may behave erratically between them. **Vicinal Risk Minimization (VRM)** (Chapelle et al., 2000) replaces each delta with a *vicinity distribution* and trains on samples drawn around each example. **mixup** is a simple, data-agnostic VRM scheme: given two random labeled examples (x_i, y_i) and (x_j, y_j) with one-hot labels, build virtual examples as convex combinations of both inputs *and* labels: **x̃ = λx_i + (1−λ)x_j, ỹ = λy_i + (1−λ)y_j**, with **λ ∼ Beta(α, α)**, α commonly in [0.1, 0.4]. Small α makes the Beta U-shaped (mixes close to one original example). Training on these interpolations enforces that the model behave **linearly between examples** — a strong, simple prior that reduces oscillation outside the training support and sensitivity to adversarial/out-of-distribution inputs, and improves calibration. Situated by Shorten & Khoshgoftaar (2019) **[in folder]** as a "mixing images" technique.
**Key reference:** Zhang, Cissé, Dauphin, Lopez-Paz, "mixup: Beyond Empirical Risk Minimization," ICLR 2018 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1710.09412.
🛠 **Hands-on:** ~10 lines: `lam = np.random.beta(a,a)`, permute the batch, mix inputs, loss = `lam*loss(pred,y) + (1-lam)*loss(pred,y_perm)`. Or `torchvision.transforms.v2.MixUp` (often with `CutMix`) on CIFAR-10; compare accuracy/calibration vs. plain ERM.
**Prerequisites:** ERM, one-hot labels & cross-entropy, data augmentation, Beta distribution, regularization.

### Deep dive #5 — Importance sampling ✅ 🎯 Advanced
Importance sampling estimates an expectation under a *target* distribution p when you can only (or prefer to) sample from a *proposal* distribution q, via a change of measure: **E_{x∼p}[f(x)] = E_{x∼q}[f(x)·p(x)/q(x)]**. The Monte Carlo estimator draws x₁,…,x_n ∼ q and averages μ̂ = (1/n)Σ f(x_i)·w_i, where **w_i = p(x_i)/q(x_i)** is the **importance weight**. It is unbiased provided q(x) > 0 wherever p(x)f(x) ≠ 0 (the *coverage* condition). The proposal q should put mass where |f(x)|p(x) is large; a mismatched q yields high-variance weights (a few samples dominate), and variance can be infinite if p/q is heavy-tailed. **Self-normalized** IS divides by Σ w_i, trading small bias for lower variance and removing the need for normalized densities. In ML this recurs: **off-policy RL** evaluates a target policy π from data under behavior policy b using weights π(a|s)/b(a|s); **dataset-shift reweighting** corrects train≠deployment via p_test(x)/p_train(x); and it underlies variance-reduction and some variational objectives. Watchword: variance control — monitor **effective sample size n_eff = (Σ w_i)² / Σ w_i²** and clip extreme weights.
**Key references:** Owen, *Monte Carlo Theory, Methods and Examples* (2013), Ch. 9 — https://artowen.su.domains/mc/. Sutton & Barto, *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction*, 2nd ed. (2018), §5.5.
🛠 **Hands-on:** In NumPy, estimate a Gaussian tail probability by sampling from a shifted proposal and reweighting; plot variance vs. proposal mean and compute n_eff. For RL, work an off-policy Monte Carlo prediction exercise from Sutton & Barto.
**Prerequisites:** expectation/integration, PDFs, Monte Carlo estimation, variance, (RL) MDPs & policies.

---

## Part 3 — Learning path (by prerequisite & difficulty)

1. **Intro:** sampling basics → nonprobability vs. random/stratified → hand vs. natural labels → class-imbalance intuition → simple augmentation.
2. **Intermediate:** weighted/reservoir sampling → natural labels & feedback loops → transfer learning → active learning → imbalance metrics (PR vs ROC) → resampling (SMOTE) → focal loss → mixup.
3. **Advanced:** importance sampling → weak-supervision label model → semi-supervision (graph/co-training) → adversarial perturbation.

## Part 4 — Cross-chapter concept graph

- ◀ **Ch 3 (Data Engineering):** data sources, formats, and streaming feed Sampling (esp. reservoir sampling) and data lineage feeds Hand Labels.
- ▶ **Ch 5 (Feature Engineering):** transfer-learning embeddings, handling missing data, and data leakage continue the "prepare the data" arc; augmentation ↔ feature transforms.
- ▶ **Ch 6 (Model Development & Offline Evaluation):** evaluation metrics (PR/ROC) and ensembles (for imbalance) are deferred here; active-learning uncertainty ↔ model confidence.
- ▶ **Ch 9 (Continual Learning & Test in Production):** natural labels, feedback-loop length, and distribution shift (the flip side of nonprobability sampling and importance reweighting).
